text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the AI Incident Response lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: AI incident response defines how staff report, triage, investigate, correct, communicate, and document problems involving AI-supported systems or outputs.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
AI incident response defines how staff report, triage, investigate, correct, communicate, and document problems involving AI-supported systems or outputs.
textPreview: AI incident response defines how staff report, triage, investigate, correct, communicate, and document problems involving AI-supported sy...

text: AI incidents can affect privacy, public trust, legal compliance, staff confidence, equity, and operational safety. A clear response pathway reduces harm and improves governance.
textPreview: AI incidents can affect privacy, public trust, legal compliance, staff confidence, equity, and operational safety. A clear response pathw...

text: Without an incident pathway, errors, bias concerns, privacy exposures, and unsafe outputs may be handled inconsistently or too late.
textPreview: Without an incident pathway, errors, bias concerns, privacy exposures, and unsafe outputs may be handled inconsistently or too late.

text:
Use this slide in the AI Incident Response training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 10, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 17, 18, 38, 44, 45, 46.
Emphasize the core guardrail: Without an incident pathway, errors, bias concerns, privacy exposures, and unsafe outputs may be handled inconsistently or too late.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain AI Incident Response in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the AI Incident Response training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 10, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 17, 18, 38, 44, 45, 46.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

AI incidents can involve incorrect outputs, privacy exposure, biased results, unsafe recommendations, unauthorized use, vendor failure, staff over-reliance, public misinformation, or model performance drift. Staff need to know what to report and what happens next.

Ask the group to restate the topic in their own words.

Harmful or inaccurate output used in a public health workflow. Sensitive data entered into an unapproved AI tool or exposed through a vendor system. Bias or disparate impact concern raised by staff, partners, or community members. Model drift,.
textPreview: Harmful or inaccurate output used in a public health workflow. Sensitive data entered into an unapproved AI tool or exposed through a ven...

text: Capture the issue, system, date, user, output, data involved, and immediate risk. Triage severity and determine whether the system should continue, be limited, paused, or shut off. Notify privacy, security, legal, program leadership,.
textPreview: Capture the issue, system, date, user, output, data involved, and immediate risk. Triage severity and determine whether the system should...

text: Train staff to report concerns without blame. Define emergency contacts and pause authority before deployment. Review incidents regularly through governance and continuous improvement.
textPreview: Train staff to report concerns without blame. Define emergency contacts and pause authority before deployment. Review incidents regularly...

text:
Use this slide in the AI Incident Response training module.
Use this slide to turn the concept into practical behaviors, decisions, and safeguards.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 10, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 17, 18, 38, 44, 45, 46.
Invite participants to identify where these practices would need to be documented in their own department.
Start with Incident Types, then compare it with the other practice areas on the slide.
Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.

text:
Use this slide in the AI Incident Response training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 3, 10, 11, 12, 13 and Tools 17, 18, 38, 44, 45, 46.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?
What AI-related problems should staff be required to report? Who has authority to pause an AI-supported workflow? How will lessons from incidents update training, policy, and monitoring?

text: Walk through a tabletop scenario involving an incorrect AI-generated public message or privacy exposure, then document reporting steps, escalation contacts, communications needs, corrective actions, and governance review.

Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: AI incident scenario response, Escalation pathway, Corrective action log.
